texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Recursive Score and Hessian Computation in Regime-Switching Models
texts.text: Recursive Score and Hessian Computation in Regime-Switching Models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chaojun Li East China Normal University Shi Qiu
texts.text: Chaojun Li East China Normal University Shi Qiu

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study proposes a recursive and easy-to-implement algorithm to compute the score and Hessian matrix in general regime-switching models. We use simulation to compare the asymptotic variance estimates constructed from the Hessian matrix and the outer product of the score. The results favor the latter.
texts.text: This study proposes a recursive and easy-to-implement algorithm to compute the score and Hessian matrix in general regime-switching models. We use simulation to compare the asymptotic variance estimates constructed from the Hessian matrix and the outer product of the score. The results favor the latter.

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regime-switching models have been widely applied since Hamilton (1989) to analyze how timeseries patterns shift across different latent economic states. These models are typically estimated via maximum likelihood, with the computation of the score vector and the Hessian matrix playing a central role in producing reliable standard errors and enabling hypothesis tests. Although Hamilton (1996) provides an algorithm for computing the score using smoothed probabilities, a direct and generally accepted method for calculating the Hessian matrix remains lacking in the literature. At the same time, inference based on the Hessian matrix is common practice. Consequently, applied research often resorts to numerical approximations for inference, as seen in studies by Psaradakis and Sola (1998) and Pouzo et al. (2022).
texts.text: Regime-switching models have been widely applied since Hamilton (1989) to analyze how timeseries patterns shift across different latent economic states. These models are typically estimated via maximum likelihood, with the computation of the score vector and the Hessian matrix playing a central role in producing reliable standard errors and enabling hypothesis tests. Although Hamilton (1996) provides an algorithm for computing the score using smoothed probabilities, a direct and generally accepted method for calculating the Hessian matrix remains lacking in the literature. At the same time, inference based on the Hessian matrix is common practice. Consequently, applied research often resorts to numerical approximations for inference, as seen in studies by Psaradakis and Sola (1998) and Pouzo et al. (2022).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The algorithm for smoothed functionals in Capp´ e et al. (2005, chapter 4.1) can, in principle, be used to compute both the score and the Hessian. However, it remains unfamiliar to most researchers for several reasons. First, its illustration has been limited to a simple one-parameter model, and although generalizable, the complete algorithm has not been laid out in a general setting. Implementing it requires a preliminary step of applying Fisher's and Louis' identities (Capp´ e et al., 2005, proposition 10.1.6) to express the score as the conditional expectation of the complete score and to relate the Hessian to that of the complete-data log-likelihood. These expressions must then be reformulated into the specific form required by the smoothing functional algorithm (Capp´ e et al., 2005, proposition 4.1.3). For a score vector of dimension k × 1 and a Hessian of size k × k , researchers must derive smoothing functionals for each element, a process that can become cumbersome and tedious.
texts.text: The algorithm for smoothed functionals in Capp´ e et al. (2005, chapter 4.1) can, in principle, be used to compute both the score and the Hessian. However, it remains unfamiliar to most researchers for several reasons. First, its illustration has been limited to a simple one-parameter model, and although generalizable, the complete algorithm has not been laid out in a general setting. Implementing it requires a preliminary step of applying Fisher's and Louis' identities (Capp´ e et al., 2005, proposition 10.1.6) to express the score as the conditional expectation of the complete score and to relate the Hessian to that of the complete-data log-likelihood. These expressions must then be reformulated into the specific form required by the smoothing functional algorithm (Capp´ e et al., 2005, proposition 4.1.3). For a score vector of dimension k × 1 and a Hessian of size k × k , researchers must derive smoothing functionals for each element, a process that can become cumbersome and tedious.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The contributions of this study are twofold. First, we propose a unified recursive framework to compute the score and Hessian matrix simultaneously. This framework is designed for a general regime-switching specification that encompasses many useful models in the empirical literature as special cases. Unlike the approach in Hamilton (1996), our algorithm does not require computing or storing smoothed probabilities and can be integrated directly into the prediction and update steps of likelihood evaluation. It is straightforward to implement, as it avoids the need for preliminary transformations via Fisher's or Louis' identities and works directly with derivatives of the period likelihood function. Researchers need only supply the first and second derivatives of certain quantities, and our algorithm returns the score and Hessian, significantly reducing pre-computation effort compared to the smoothed functional approach. Second, to our knowledge, no thorough investigation has been conducted to compare inference based on the exact score versus the exact Hessian in this context. We supplement our methodological contribution with simulation evidence to examine their finite-sample properties.
texts.text: The contributions of this study are twofold. First, we propose a unified recursive framework to compute the score and Hessian matrix simultaneously. This framework is designed for a general regime-switching specification that encompasses many useful models in the empirical literature as special cases. Unlike the approach in Hamilton (1996), our algorithm does not require computing or storing smoothed probabilities and can be integrated directly into the prediction and update steps of likelihood evaluation. It is straightforward to implement, as it avoids the need for preliminary transformations via Fisher's or Louis' identities and works directly with derivatives of the period likelihood function. Researchers need only supply the first and second derivatives of certain quantities, and our algorithm returns the score and Hessian, significantly reducing pre-computation effort compared to the smoothed functional approach. Second, to our knowledge, no thorough investigation has been conducted to compare inference based on the exact score versus the exact Hessian in this context. We supplement our methodological contribution with simulation evidence to examine their finite-sample properties.

texts.text: Notations: let Y n m ≜ ( Y m , . . . , Y n ) ′ for m ≤ n , and let Y n m be an empty set for m > n . Similarly, define S n m and X n m . We use N ( µ, σ 2 ) to denote the density of the normal distribution with mean µ and variance σ 2 .

texts.orig: where p, q ≥ 0, X t is a predetermined variable (vector), g θ is a family of density functions indexed by θ . The regime S t is a first-order Markov chain on S = { 1 , 2 , . . . , J } with transition probability q ij = q θ ( S t = j | S t -1 = i ) for i, j = 1 , . . . , J . The regime S t is a first-order Markov chain on S = { 1 , 2 , . . . , J } with transition probability q ij = q θ ( S t = j | S t -1 = i ) for i, j = 1 , . . . , J . Given ( S t -1 t -max { p, 1 } , Y t -1 t -q , X t ), ( Y t , S t ) is independent of ( { Y k } k ≤ t -q -1 , { S k } k ≤ t -max { p,q } , { X k } k ≤ t -1 ), and the conditional density can be written as

Its first and second derivatives are expressed as ∇ θ f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ) and ∇ 2 θ f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ).
texts.text: For short notation, let S t ≜ S t t -max { p, 1 } +1 . We abbreviate the dependence on Y t , Y t -1 , and X t into the subscript t . Then (1) can be expressed as f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ). Its first and second derivatives are expressed as ∇ θ f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ) and ∇ 2 θ f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Algorithm to compute score and Hessian
texts.text: 3 Algorithm to compute score and Hessian

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can compute the score and Hessian matrix if we know how to compute (5)-(6) and (8)-(9). (8) can be computed as (6) by replacing ∇ θ f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ) with ∇ 2 θ f θ,t ( S t , S t -1 ). Thus, the main challenge is to compute (5), (6), and (9) efficiently. To explain the idea of the algorithm, we first consider the simple model where there is no regime switching in Section 3.1.

To explain the idea of the algorithm, we first consider the simple model where there is no regime switching in Section 3.1.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2 The algorithm to compute the score and Hessian matrix
texts.text: 3.2 The algorithm to compute the score and Hessian matrix

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Section 3.1 explains the algorithm to compute (5), (6), and (9) in the model without regime switching. To obtain the complete algorithm for the model with regime switching, we need to make two modifications. First, for models with regime switching, we need to take summation over regimes at appropriate steps. For each regime S t , it is involved in f θ,k ( S k , S k -1 ), t ≤ k ≤ t + p , but not in f θ,ℓ ( S ℓ , S ℓ -1 ), ℓ ≥ t + p +1. Thus, we can take summation over S t at the recursion step of t + p . Second, in (4) and (7), the computation of the denominator p θ,n = p θ,ν ( Y 1 , . . . , Y n | Y 0 , X n 1 ) might suffer from numerical overflow or underflow. 1 Thus, instead of making a division in the last step, we re-scale each value with the period likelihood at each recursion step. From p θ,t = ∏ t k =1 p θ,ν ( Y k | Y k -1 0 , X k 1 ), we can re-scale at each recursion with p θ,ν ( Y k | Y k -1 0 , X k 1 ). The complete algorithm can be described as follows.
texts.text: Section 3.1 explains the algorithm to compute (5), (6), and (9) in the model without regime switching. To obtain the complete algorithm for the model with regime switching, we need to make two modifications. First, for models with regime switching, we need to take summation over regimes at appropriate steps. For each regime S t , it is involved in f θ,k ( S k , S k -1 ), t ≤ k ≤ t + p , but not in f θ,ℓ ( S ℓ , S ℓ -1 ), ℓ ≥ t + p +1. Thus, we can take summation over S t at the recursion step of t + p . Second, in (4) and (7), the computation of the denominator p θ,n = p θ,ν ( Y 1 , . . . , Y n | Y 0 , X n 1 ) might suffer from numerical overflow or underflow. 1 Thus, instead of making a division in the last step, we re-scale each value with the period likelihood at each recursion step. From p θ,t = ∏ t k =1 p θ,ν ( Y k | Y k -1 0 , X k 1 ), we can re-scale at each recursion with p θ,ν ( Y k | Y k -1 0 , X k 1 ). The complete algorithm can be described as follows.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 1 To see this more clearly, note that under suitable regularity conditions, the normalized log-likelihood satisfies n -1 log p θ,ν ( Y 1 , . . . , Y n | Y 0 , X n 1 ) p -→ ℓ ( θ ) in the consistency proof; see Douc et al. (2004) and Kasahara and Shimotsu (2019). Consequently, for large n , the likelihood itself behaves as p θ,ν ( Y 1 , . . . , Y n | Y 0 , X n 1 ) ≈ exp ( nℓ ( θ ) ) . If ℓ ( θ ) < 0, then exp( nℓ ( θ )) decays exponentially to zero. Once its magnitude falls below the machine epsilon, floating-point software will round it to zero, resulting in numerical underflow. Conversely, if ℓ ( θ ) > 0, the term grows exponentially and may exceed the largest representable floating-point number, causing numerical overflow (stored as INF).
texts.text: 1 To see this more clearly, note that under suitable regularity conditions, the normalized log-likelihood satisfies n -1 log p θ,ν ( Y 1 , . . . , Y n | Y 0 , X n 1 ) p -→ ℓ ( θ ) in the consistency proof; see Douc et al. (2004) and Kasahara and Shimotsu (2019). Consequently, for large n , the likelihood itself behaves as p θ,ν ( Y 1 , . . . , Y n | Y 0 , X n 1 ) ≈ exp ( nℓ ( θ ) ) . If ℓ ( θ ) < 0, then exp( nℓ ( θ )) decays exponentially to zero. Once its magnitude falls below the machine epsilon, floating-point software will round it to zero, resulting in numerical underflow. Conversely, if ℓ ( θ ) > 0, the term grows exponentially and may exceed the largest representable floating-point number, causing numerical overflow (stored as INF).

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Unlike the smoothed functional approach in Hamilton (1996), our algorithm is recursive and does not require the computation and storage of the smoothed probabilities. The connection between our algorithm and the standard prediction-and-update steps for evaluating the likelihood function can be seen as follows. For t ≥ 2, p θ,t ( S t ) = p θ,ν ( Y t , S t | Y t -1 0 , X t 1 ) ∑ S t p θ,t ( S t ) = p θ,ν ( Y t | Y t -1 0 , X t 1 ) are the terms computed in the prediction step. ˜ p θ,t ( S t ) = p θ,ν ( S t | Y t 0 , X t 1 ) is the term computed in the updating step. Thus, the recursion in this algorithm extends the standard prediction and update algorithm of likelihood evaluation to compute the score and the Hessian matrix simultaneously.

texts.text: For each model, we generate 1000 data sets of sample sizes n =100, 200, 400, and 800. 2 For each data set, we compute the MLE using the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno optimization method and compute the standard errors based on (14) and (15).

texts.orig: We report the coverage frequencies of the 95% confidence intervals in Table 1 and the ratio of the standard deviation of the MLE to the median of the standard errors across 1000 datasets in Table 2 for M µ and M σ . The asymptotic normality provides a better approximation in M µ than M σ .

The asymptotic normality provides a better approximation in M µ than M σ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More specifically, the coverage frequencies are closer to the nominal level of 95% and the ratios of sampling standard deviations to the estimated standard errors are closer to unity in the model M µ than M σ . The performance in the remaining models falls between these two cases. To save space, we present only results for M µ and M σ . Full results are available upon request. When n = 100, the performance of the asymptotic normal distribution is somewhat discouraging. The coverage frequencies are lower than the nominal level of 95%, and the estimated standard errors are smaller than the sampling standard deviations of the MLE. When n gets larger to 800, the performance improves. Moreover, inference based on the outer product of the score (15) outperforms that based on (14), in the sense that coverage frequencies are closer to 95% and the ratio of standard deviations to standard errors is closer to unity. This pattern holds across all models examined.
texts.text: More specifically, the coverage frequencies are closer to the nominal level of 95% and the ratios of sampling standard deviations to the estimated standard errors are closer to unity in the model M µ than M σ . The performance in the remaining models falls between these two cases. To save space, we present only results for M µ and M σ . Full results are available upon request. When n = 100, the performance of the asymptotic normal distribution is somewhat discouraging. The coverage frequencies are lower than the nominal level of 95%, and the estimated standard errors are smaller than the sampling standard deviations of the MLE. When n gets larger to 800, the performance improves. Moreover, inference based on the outer product of the score (15) outperforms that based on (14), in the sense that coverage frequencies are closer to 95% and the ratio of standard deviations to standard errors is closer to unity. This pattern holds across all models examined.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study proposes a recursive algorithm for computing the score vector and Hessian matrix in regime-switching models, without relying on pre-computation via Fisher's or Louis' identities. Simulation results show that asymptotic approximations based on the outer product of the score are favored over those derived from the Hessian matrix. While the presented framework is general, it does not encompass more complex specifications such as regime-switching GARCH models or regime-switching state-space models, where likelihood evaluation requires approximation. Extending the algorithm to compute score and Hessian matrix for such models will be addressed in future work.
texts.text: This study proposes a recursive algorithm for computing the score vector and Hessian matrix in regime-switching models, without relying on pre-computation via Fisher's or Louis' identities. Simulation results show that asymptotic approximations based on the outer product of the score are favored over those derived from the Hessian matrix. While the presented framework is general, it does not encompass more complex specifications such as regime-switching GARCH models or regime-switching state-space models, where likelihood evaluation requires approximation. Extending the algorithm to compute score and Hessian matrix for such models will be addressed in future work.
